abstract.text: Managing state across a sequence of related views.

inlineContent.text: Onboarding wizards, multi-step forms, guided checkout. They all share two awkward facts: each step needs to see what previous steps collected, and “done” means tearing down every screen the sequence pushed.
Solving that with raw
and a chain of
hand-offs forces the state to live somewhere uncomfortable. Usually that’s in a parent view or a view model that outlives the sequence and has no business knowing about it. Drop in an
and you’ve added a shared mutable container to the environment for a workflow that only existed for thirty seconds.
collapses both problems into one type. A struct that owns the data the sequence collects, and also which also uses that knowledge to determine which step comes next.
This makes the flow state dependent.
Just like SwiftUI wants.
The protocol is small. An associated
, a checkpoint property that Navigator manages for you, and two methods that drive the sequence forward.
is
because deciding what comes next may need to await a network call or surface an error. It returns a tuple of
so the flow can hand back an updated copy of itself alongside the navigation decision. The three lifecycle hooks have empty default implementations. Override only the ones you care about.
Here’s a real flow: an onboarding sequence that collects a name and email and fires a completion callback when the user finishes.

code: private let completion: Callback<(String, String, String)>

inlineContent.text: Notice what isn’t there. No
. No class. No
. No shared store. The flow is a plain struct. The flow
is
its state.
That’s the part worth repeating. The flow struct holds the data the sequence collects, and it travels through the destination enum as an associated value.
Each step receives the current flow, mutates its own fields, and hands the updated copy back to the navigator.
is
, so the button wraps it in a
and either handles or swallows any error the flow raises.
There’s no view model. There’s no shared store. The
binds straight into the flow’s stored properties via
‘s projected binding, and the button hands the mutated copy back to the navigator. That’s the entire data layer for this screen.

Value semantics are doing real work here. Each pushed step gets its own copy of the flow as it existed at push time, so navigating back doesn’t observe later mutations, and SwiftUI’s diff over the navigation path behaves exactly the way it does for any other
value. The flow is just data in the path.
Two methods on
move a flow forward.
stamps a checkpoint capturing the current navigator and its path index, then calls the flow’s
and routes the result.
calls
and routes the result.

on the protocol is synchronous.

is, so callers wrap it in a
. Both route the returned
the same way:
pushes the next step, and
, or
returns the stack to the checkpoint and fires the corresponding hook.
The flow’s
is the state machine. It inspects the current fields, optionally does asynchronous work, and returns the updated flow alongside the navigation decision.
Each branch reads a piece of state and returns the unchanged flow alongside the next destination. Notice that
never returns
here. The flow’s last screen always pushes a confirmation view, and terminating the flow is the confirmation view’s job, not the state machine’s. Past five or six steps this chain of
-checks turns into a debugging puzzle and an explicit step enum is the right refactor.
The flow needs to do
something
with the data it collects. The naive answer is to hand the handler into the final view as a parameter and let the view call it.
Don’t do that.

The completion screen has no business knowing that a callback exists. It wants to know how to dismiss the flow when the user taps Done. The callback, the arguments, the data the callback needs, none of that is the view’s problem.
So the closure goes on the flow, private. And the flow fires it from one of the lifecycle hooks the protocol already gives you.

code: private let completion: Callback<(String, String, String)>

inlineContent.text: is exactly what it sounds like. A hook that fires after the flow completes. The view calls
, the navigator restores the stack to the checkpoint, then calls the flow’s
, which fires the closure with the data the flow has been accumulating.
This is the division the protocol is designed around.
and
are navigation decisions. The hooks are where side effects go. Don’t reach for the view layer to invoke a callback the flow already has all the information to invoke itself.
requires
so the flow can ride the navigation path as part of a destination enum’s associated value. The moment you store a raw closure on the flow you lose the auto-synthesized
conformance, because closures aren’t
You could implement
by hand, list every value property in
and
, and skip the closure. That works. But Navigator ships
for exactly this case.

code: // ... hashes on identifier, calls handler via callAsFunction

inlineContent.text: wraps a closure with a stable identifier (a fresh UUID by default, or one you pass in). Equality and hashing operate on the identifier instead of the closure itself, so the wrapping flow keeps its synthesized

conformance with no boilerplate. The trade is that two callbacks created with the same closure compare unequal, which is the right semantic when the closure is constructed once at flow init and never replaced.
Wrap the incoming closure in the flow’s initializer:
And invoke it via
, which lets you call the wrapper like a closure:
The single tuple argument matches Callback’s
type parameter.
is not
. Storing one on a flow disables state restoration for any

that hosts the flow, and it can interfere with deep linking because external URL handlers can’t synthesize the underlying closure. When state restoration or deep-link reachability matters more than a one-shot callback, return the collected value through a checkpoint instead. See
The previous example’s
is synchronous because it doesn’t need to await anything. Real flows often do.
is
so it can

a network call, a persistence write, or any other effect that produces information needed to decide which step comes next. The tuple return is what makes that ergonomic: the method hands back an updated copy of the flow alongside the navigation result, so whatever the async work produced is captured on the flow as it advances.

code: let userID = try await api.register(email: email, name: firstName)

inlineContent.text: is a default-implemented helper on
that takes a closure, applies it to a mutable copy of
, and returns the result. It exists specifically so that returning updated state from a non-mutating method stays a one-liner.
If the async work throws, the error propagates out through
. The view’s
swallows it. If you’d rather treat a failure as fatal to the flow, return
from
instead. The navigator routes that to
after restoring the stack to the checkpoint, the same way it routes
to
The async work happens before any navigation occurs. The user sees the last screen they were on until
returns. If the work might take a moment, the calling view should reflect that, usually by disabling its action button or showing a progress indicator while its
is in flight.
A flow can mix presentation methods across its steps. The first step opens as a sheet, the rest push within it.
The checkpoint is recorded relative to the navigator that called
, so completion dismisses the sheet
and
pops back to whatever pushed the start button. One mechanism, no manual coordination between push depth and sheet presentation.
See
for the full picture of how Navigator tears down nested presentations.
A flow ends one of two ways. Either
returns one of the terminal
cases (
), or a view calls the equivalent method on the navigator directly (
). Both routes do the same thing: restore the navigation stack to the checkpoint, then call the matching hook on the flow.
The onboarding example uses the imperative form. The confirmation screen knows it’s the last step, so it doesn’t need the state machine to figure that out.

code: Text("That's all there is to it!")

inlineContent.text: The hooks themselves have empty default implementations.
Override only what you need. A welcome toast in
. Telemetry in
. Crash reporting in
The hooks fire
after
the navigator has returned to the checkpoint, not before. If

wants to show a confirmation, the user is already back at the entry point, not on the final step of the flow. That’s almost always what you want, but it’s worth being explicit about so you don’t try to push something onto a stack that no longer exists.
We can go further.
is a protocol, but flows themselves can
also
be protocols.
Why? What if we wanted to reuse a screen across different sequences. An “address entry” view that belongs in both checkout and profile editing doesn’t want to be hard-coded to one concrete flow type.
The view is generic over any flow that satisfies the requirement. Both
and
route their address step through the same implementation. Each flow still declares its own
enum and decides which step comes when. Only the screen is shared.

content.text: When Not to Use a Flow

inlineContent.text: A two-screen interaction that just hands a value back doesn’t justify a flow. A named checkpoint with a typed return value is lighter and more direct. See

Reach for a flow when the sequence accumulates state across three or more steps, when the same sequence needs to start from multiple entry points in the app, or when the sequence ends in an effect that needs the accumulated state in one place.
